question: What time does AI6130 meet each week?
gold_answer: The course meets every week from 6:30 pm to 9:30 pm.

question: Where are the course materials uploaded according to the lecture?
gold_answer: The course materials are uploaded on the GitHub page https://github.com/ntu-nail/AI6130.

question: What programming language and framework are required for the class assignments?
gold_answer: All class assignments will be in Python and PyTorch.

question: What is one stated course objective related to LLM skills?
gold_answer: One course objective is to gain skills in developing, fine-tuning, and deploying LLMs.

question: What percentage of the grade comes from the final group-based project?
gold_answer: The final group-based project counts for 50% of the grade.

question: When will Assignment 1 be released?
gold_answer: Assignment 1 will be up on the 6th week.

question: What kind of topic can students choose for the final project?
gold_answer: Students can choose an application of LLMs for real-world applications, or a new architecture or technique for training or finetuning LLMs.

question: According to the lecture, what is one key advantage of deep learning over manually designed features?
gold_answer: Learned features are easy to adapt and fast to learn.

question: What happens in the LLM era according to the history of deep learning slide?
gold_answer: In the LLM era, a single huge model is trained on astronomical amounts of data and then prompted for everything.

question: What model size range is listed for LLMs in the parameter comparison table?
gold_answer: The lecture lists LLMs as ranging from 7B to 1T parameters.

question: According to the beam search slide, what assumption does beam search make about the best sequence?
gold_answer: Beam search assumes the best possible sequence is the one with the highest overall sequence likelihood according to the model.

question: Why does the lecture say beam search is used instead of exhaustive search?
gold_answer: It is used because searching all possible sequences for the most likely one is computationally intractable.

question: What model family is presented on page 27 of Lecture 3?
gold_answer: Page 27 presents RNN as a language model.

question: What training objective is highlighted on pages 31 to 35 of Lecture 3?
gold_answer: The highlighted training objective is negative log likelihood.

question: What type of model is introduced on pages 28 and 29?
gold_answer: The lecture introduces sequence-to-sequence models.

question: What topic is introduced on page 36 before decoding examples?
gold_answer: Page 36 introduces how generation is done.

question: What issue is explicitly named on page 54 of the lecture?
gold_answer: Page 54 is about problems with beam search.

question: What decision topic is covered on page 55 after discussing beam search problems?
gold_answer: Page 55 discusses when to use beam search.

question: What distinction is introduced on pages 10 to 13 of the lecture?
gold_answer: The lecture introduces the distinction between conditioned and unconditioned neural language models.

question: What earlier language modeling approach appears on page 5?
gold_answer: Page 5 presents the n-gram based approach to language modeling.

question: In the transformer overview, what comes immediately after the self-attention layer in the encoder?
gold_answer: The outputs of the self-attention layer are fed to a feed-forward neural network.

question: How does the lecture say order is injected into embeddings?
gold_answer: Order is injected by adding positional encoding vectors whose values follow a sine/cosine pattern.

question: What does layer normalization do according to the residual slide?
gold_answer: Layer normalization changes the input to have mean 0 and variance 1, per layer and per training point.

question: What is one feed-forward activation variant explicitly named in the lecture?
gold_answer: GELU is one explicitly named feed-forward activation variant.

question: What benefit is reported for bilinear FFNs with reduced hidden dimension?
gold_answer: They improve the speed-quality tradeoff while keeping the parameter count the same.

question: How does grouped-query attention reduce cache cost?
gold_answer: It divides Q into groups and shares K and V within each group, reducing K and V cache storage to the group size.

question: What is multi-query attention in this lecture?
gold_answer: Multi-query attention uses a single K and V for all Q heads.

question: What stability issue is associated with Post-LN at large scale?
gold_answer: Post-LN is associated with large gradient L2 norm and unstable training at large scale.

question: What is RMSNorm according to the layer norm slides?
gold_answer: RMSNorm performs only rescaling and no recentering.

question: What does the lecture say is the main value of positional encoding?
gold_answer: Positional encoding adds positional information, which is useful for language, because the transformer itself is position agnostic.

question: Why does the lecture motivate pre-training for NLP tasks like parsing, translation, and QA?
gold_answer: It says these tasks all need expensive annotation, motivating unsupervised training.

question: Why is next word prediction described as unsupervised?
gold_answer: Because next word prediction is inherently an unsupervised task.

question: What architecture does OpenAI GPT use in the lecture?
gold_answer: OpenAI GPT uses a decoder-only Transformer architecture.

question: What problem with unidirectional language models is illustrated by the word 'bank' example?
gold_answer: The lecture says language understanding is bidirectional, so the meaning of 'bank' depends on right context such as 'to deposit money'.

question: How large is BERT Base according to the lecture?
gold_answer: BERT Base has 12 Transformer blocks, 768 hidden units, 12 attention heads, and 110M parameters.

question: What percentage of tokens are masked in BERT masked language modeling?
gold_answer: BERT masks 15% of the input text.

question: What does next sentence prediction ask BERT to decide?
gold_answer: It asks whether sentence B follows sentence A in the training corpus.

question: What is one major design change made by RoBERTa compared with BERT?
gold_answer: RoBERTa removes the NSP task and uses longer sequences.

question: How does ALBERT reduce embedding parameters?
gold_answer: ALBERT uses factorized embedding parametrization, decomposing the embedding instead of using a large V x H matrix directly.

question: What positional embedding choice is listed for Llama-2 pretraining?
gold_answer: Llama-2 uses rotary positional embeddings instead of absolute positional embeddings.

question: What was the older NLP paradigm described at the start of Lecture 6?
gold_answer: The older paradigm was one model per task trained exclusively on task-specific data.

question: What paradigm shift around 2018 does the lecture describe?
gold_answer: The shift was to pre-train one language model for generic language understanding and then finetune it for each task.

question: What pretraining objective is associated with GPT-1 in the lecture?
gold_answer: GPT-1 is described as a decoder-only language model pre-trained with a language modeling objective.

question: Why is full model finetuning expensive for modern LLMs?
gold_answer: Because training separately on each task is expensive and storing many model checkpoints is also expensive.

question: What three efficiency solutions are listed after the disadvantages of full finetuning?
gold_answer: The lecture lists in-context learning, parameter-efficient finetuning, and multi-task finetuning leading to instruction finetuning.

question: How does the lecture define zero-shot learning in in-context learning?
gold_answer: Zero-shot learning is a prompt that contains task instructions but no examples of the task being performed.

question: How does the lecture define few-shot learning in in-context learning?
gold_answer: Few-shot learning is a prompt that contains both instructions and several examples of the task being performed.

question: What is a verbalizer according to the summary slide?
gold_answer: A verbalizer is the template we wrap an example in to perform the task.

question: What is the core idea of prompt tuning?
gold_answer: Instead of optimizing discrete prompt tokens, prompt tuning optimizes a sequence of learnable embeddings prepended to the query.

question: How does prefix tuning differ from prompt tuning?
gold_answer: Prefix tuning appends the learned prefix not only to the input embeddings but at each Transformer layer.

question: What key weakness of pretrained language models motivates instruction tuning?
gold_answer: They are not good at following human-provided instructions at inference time.

question: What are the two common mitigations mentioned before instruction tuning?
gold_answer: The lecture lists fine-tuning and few-shot prompting.

question: What is instruction tuning?
gold_answer: Instruction tuning finetunes a language model on many tasks described via instructions so the model learns to follow instructions and generalize zero-shot.

question: How many NLP datasets were used in FLAN according to the lecture?
gold_answer: FLAN used 62 NLP datasets grouped into 12 clusters.

question: What is the base model for FLAN?
gold_answer: The base model is LaMDA-PT, a decoder-only model with 137B parameters.

question: What dataset cap is used when training FLAN?
gold_answer: FLAN training caps each dataset at 30k examples.

question: What kind of task needs answer options when using the decoder-only base model in FLAN?
gold_answer: Classification tasks need options.

question: What is the base model used for T0?
gold_answer: T0 uses an LM-adapted variation of T5 called LM+T5.

question: According to the T0 training slide, how many total tokens are used in T0 training?
gold_answer: T0 training uses a total of 250B tokens.

question: What is LIMA's central hypothesis about instruction tuning data?
gold_answer: LIMA hypothesizes that only limited instruction tuning data is necessary to teach models to produce high-quality output.

question: What is the goal of alignment according to Lecture 8?
gold_answer: The goal of alignment is to build AI systems that are aligned with human values: helpful, honest, and harmless.

question: In the RLHF framing, what is the state and what is the action?
gold_answer: In RLHF, the prompt is the state and the generation is the action.

question: What kind of data is used to train the supervised fine-tuning model in RLHF?
gold_answer: It uses human-written demonstrations of the desired output behaviour.

question: How are binary preferences turned into rewards in RLHF according to the lecture?
gold_answer: A reward model is fine-tuned to output rankings consistent with human preferences, and Bradley-Terry turns that reward model into a binary preference classifier.

question: What does the lecture say Bradley-Terry reduces to on the reward modeling slides?
gold_answer: The lecture says it is just a softmax.

question: What extra term constrains RLHF optimization besides reward maximization?
gold_answer: RLHF adds a KL-divergence penalty to keep the optimized model close to a reference model.

question: Why is the KL penalty needed in RLHF?
gold_answer: It is needed to keep the optimized model from drifting too far, because the reward model is only a proxy and otherwise reward values can lose correlation with human judgments.

question: Which RL algorithm does the lecture say is used in practice for this flavor of RLHF?
gold_answer: The lecture says Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is used in practice.

question: What is the key motivation for DPO over PPO-based RLHF?
gold_answer: DPO aims to skip reward modeling and update the model directly with preference data because PPO is notoriously difficult to get right.

question: What is RLAIF?
gold_answer: RLAIF is reinforcement learning from AI feedback, where an aligned language model provides feedback instead of humans.

question: How does the lecture characterize prompt engineering?
gold_answer: Prompt engineering is finding an effective way to communicate with AI tools.

question: What is the main idea of chain-of-thought prompting?
gold_answer: Chain-of-thought prompting asks the model to produce a series of intermediate reasoning steps.

question: What kind of tasks does standard prompting work poorly on according to the comparison slide?
gold_answer: It works poorly on tasks that require reasoning abilities.

question: What phrase is given as an example of step-by-step guidance in CoT prompting?
gold_answer: The example phrase is 'Let's do it step by step.'

question: What is one attractive property of chain-of-thought prompting?
gold_answer: It enables decomposition of multi-step problems.

question: What benchmark datasets are listed for arithmetic reasoning evaluation of CoT?
gold_answer: The lecture lists GSM8K, SVAMP, ASDiV, AQuA, and MAWPS.

question: What does the lecture say about CoT and model scale?
gold_answer: It says CoT is an emergent ability of model scale and has no effect on small models.

question: What are the three steps of self-consistency?
gold_answer: They are prompting the LLM with CoT, sampling diverse reasoning paths, and selecting the most consistent answer such as by majority voting.

question: Why is Program of Thoughts motivated?
gold_answer: It is motivated by the idea that computation should be delegated to a program interpreter instead of forcing the language model to do the math itself.

question: What limitation of least-to-most prompting is stated in the lecture?
gold_answer: Decomposition prompts typically do not generalize well across different domains.

question: What is a retrieval-augmented language model according to the definitions slide?
gold_answer: It is a language model that can use large-scale text data from a datastore at inference time.

question: What limitation of parametric LLMs is emphasized before introducing retrieval?
gold_answer: They can hallucinate because they cannot memorize everything in their parameters.

question: How does retrieval-augmented generation reduce hallucinations?
gold_answer: It retrieves external documents, which especially helps on long-tail knowledge and reduces hallucinations.

question: What adaptation benefit do retrieval-augmented LMs provide without retraining?
gold_answer: They can adapt to new information by swapping datastores instead of continuously retraining model parameters.

question: What attribution benefit is mentioned for retrieval-augmented LMs?
gold_answer: They can provide a small number of retrieved documents as attributions.

question: What data-governance benefit is described as flexible data opt-in/out?
gold_answer: High-risk data can be incorporated or removed dynamically at inference time rather than training time.

question: Which early open-domain QA system from 2017 is listed in the history timeline?
gold_answer: DrQA is the 2017 system listed in the timeline.

question: What is input augmentation in retrieval-augmented LMs?
gold_answer: Input augmentation means augmenting the input of the language model with retrieved context.

question: What core idea of RETRO is highlighted in the lecture?
gold_answer: RETRO incorporates retrieved context in intermediate transformer layers using cross-attention, enabling more efficient use of many documents.

question: How does kNN-LM combine retrieved information with the base LM?
gold_answer: kNN-LM interpolates the LM's parametric next-token distribution with a nonparametric distribution from similar training contexts.

question: What three efficient inference topics are covered in the outline?
gold_answer: The lecture covers speculative decoding, paged attention, and flash attention.

question: Why is serving large LLMs described as costly?
gold_answer: One example given is that a single model instance requiring 8 A100 GPUs costs more than $10 per hour.

question: What business challenge of LLM serving is named on the early slides?
gold_answer: The lecture says missing economies of scale makes LLM serving a huge challenge for real business feasibility.

question: What is the tradeoff of distillation, pruning, quantization, and early exit according to the lecture?
gold_answer: They make the LLM smaller but inevitably lose some model capability.

question: What is the core setup of speculative decoding?
gold_answer: A smaller model proposes tokens and a larger model verifies them.

question: What makes speculative decoding attractive in practice?
gold_answer: It can yield major efficiency gains without trading away effectiveness, though it makes the system more complicated.

question: What is identified as the main bottleneck in LLM serving?
gold_answer: Typical LLM serving is KV-cache memory bound, so GPU memory becomes the bottleneck before factors like FLOPs.

question: What is the key idea of paged attention in vLLM?
gold_answer: It splits a sequence's KV cache into blocks for more flexible allocation, similar to classic paging in CPU memory management.

question: What performance benefit of paged attention is stated?
gold_answer: PagedAttention eliminates memory waste from fragmentation and allows significantly more requests per batch.

question: What is flash attention optimizing according to the lecture?
gold_answer: Flash attention improves performance by reducing high-bandwidth memory IO through blockwise attention computation in fast SRAM.
